title: Is the Fed Tightening Now So It Can Ease Even More Later?

The stock market just hit a new all-time high. The 401 K's just hit a new all-time high. And oil is dropping like a rock. Other than that, it's another day in paradise. >> President Trump touting new market records at the White House yesterday. And in fact, this morning futures are operating on a half day of trading. They are pointing to a decline with the NASDAQ down 93 95 right now after a huge rally in stocks since the end of March. President Trump is spot-on. Wall Street and bond markets are closed today for the Juneteenth federal holiday. Yesterday, all three major indices finished higher across the board with the NASDAQ up almost 500 points. It was up 496 at the close. That was almost 2%. The S&P was up better than 1%. Yesterday, this close was higher across the board, but nine Federal Reserve officials did point to the possibility of an increase in interest rates later this year. Uh they markets certainly responded to that after the war meeting earlier in the week. Markets this week had a good one. Take a look. Green across the board on the week. The Dow Jones Industrial Average was up 3/4 of a percent on the week. The NASDAQ up 2 and 1/2 percent and the S&P 500 higher by 1% for the week. Oil prices this morning trading this way. Take a look as planned talks in Switzerland over the Iran deal are postponed. Vice President J.D. Vance will not travel to Switzerland today. But the VP said that 12 and 1/2 million barrels of oil moved through the Strait of Hormuz already in one day. The price of crude closed yesterday at 76.85. Joining me now is 42 Macro Founder and CEO Darius Dale. Darius, good to see you. Thanks so much for being here. What do you think of this new development for the Iran war? Does that impact the way you're investing? >> Well, happy Juneteenth, Maria. Thank you for having me. The answer is simple. The answer is no. In our opinion, we think that's a a sideshow relative to the big event this week, uh which is all the sea change in Fed monetary policy. Uh Kevin Warsh outlined a lot of change uh that I think investors are going to need to grapple with over the in in the incoming months. >> Yeah, I mean, the markets are up since the end of March. Take a look at this, the Nasdaq's up 27.5% since March 31st, the S&P up 18% and the Dow up 14%. To what do you attribute this strength? Of course, led by AI stocks, which rallied again yesterday. Look at this, Micron up better than 8% yesterday, Taiwan Semiconductor up better than 6%, Intel rallied as well after President Trump confirmed that Apple has agreed to partner with Intel to design and build chips in the United States. And and and by the way, Intel stock has surged uh ever since the federal government took a 10% stake in the company last August. The stock is up 520% in 1 year. It's up 270% year-to-date. SpaceX finished 4% lower yesterday as we await more massive IPOs this year, but SpaceX is also up 14% since its debut. Remember, it went public at 135, it's sitting at 185 and change right now. So, Darius, what's your take on this resilience in markets led by tech? >> Well, thanks for asking that question, Maria, in the way that you did because you asked me to what do I attribute this strength, and it's exactly everything that you and I have been talking about for for well over a year now. Recall that at the height of the tariff mania last in April last year, I was telling you and your viewers that, "Hey, you need to put earmuffs on and focus on what we were calling paradigm C, which was the administration's choice to try to outgrow the debt trajectory with a pro-growth policy mix." We are in a rare confluence in a in an economic expansion with fiscal easing, monetary easing, and regulatory easing. And then I came back on your show a few months after that in the fall and said, "Hey, I think this thing is going to bubble uh because ultimately we're layering on incremental monetary easing in the form of the Fed's balance sheet expansion." So, this bubble is something that we've been calling for and it's something that we don't think is over. >> Well, I mean, look, this this chart really uh struck me yesterday when I saw money market assets opt to a record $7.9 trillion. Government prime tax-exempt money market funds all higher, but now we're talking about almost $8 trillion in money market funds. Now, the traditional thinking is that when you've got that kind of cash sitting in money market accounts, oftentimes it moves right into the stock market overall. Look at this chart showing how uh people have been putting money in their money market accounts. Do you believe that ultimately money market funds going higher means ultimately that money will, in fact, move into the stock market? >> Uh yes, but not until the market uh crashes. Unfortunately, history shows that the money market funds typically only exit and rotate into the stock market after a market crash, after a recession. So, if you're bullish, uh this is not the catalyst for you, but in our opinion, we think there are a lot of bullish catalysts that still lie ahead. Uh one, we If you think about Kevin Warsh's task force that he outlined on Wednesday, if you think about the five, uh only one of them is likely to result in a in a in a negative outcome from the perspective of the economy and financial markets, and that's the communications task force. When you think about the balance sheet task force, of course, Kevin Warsh wants a smaller balance sheet, but he's likely to couple that uh with banking sector deregulation, so it's only going to be marginally hawkish and and likely take a very long time to play out. Uh but the other three task force, particularly data, productivity and jobs, and inflation, the net result of these task force, in our opinion, based on our understanding and interpretation of the data and the associated economic and geopolitical risks, is that that those are going to send dovish signals to the members of the FOMC in about 2 to 3 quarters time. If you think about it from a data perspective, a a a real a better real-time read on the labor market will make the Fed understand that productivity growth is actually higher than they realize because the labor market's been worse uh than than than the jobs reports have been than than been reporting in real time. >> Yeah, but what about the prediction markets? I mean, Kalshi traders say that there's a better than 50% chance that the Federal Reserve does in fact raise interest rates this year. And that's after those nine Fed officials projected at least one rate hike later on this year, Darius. Is that what you're expecting? >> Yeah, that is what we're expecting. It's what we've been calling for. So, we published a note to our global investor community, a report yesterday titled "How long will the Fed pretend to be hawkish?" And in our estimation, based on our interpretation of the result of these task forces, that if they're only going to pretend to be hawkish for about two to three quarters. Now, we do think they'll get in at least one rate hike this year, probably two, but at the end of the day, that creates a buying opportunity in what we've been calling the 42 macro paradigm C bull market that is likely to extend itself much higher from from perhaps a lower price this summer, but the reality is is where this bull market is not done in our in our view. >> All right, we will be watching all of that. Darius, good to see this morning. Thanks so much. >> Yeah, thank you for having me, Maria. >> All right.
